quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Power"?

options.text: P = dW/dt = F·v; the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred, measured in watts (W) = joules/second
Friction converts kinetic energy to thermal energy; work done by friction equals -f_k × d, always negative
J = ∫F dt = Δp; the integral of force over time equals the change in momentum; measured in N·s
A dimensionless vector of magnitude 1 indicating direction; î, ĵ, k̂ denote the x, y, z directions respectively

quiz_questions.explanation: "Power" is defined as: P = dW/dt = F·v; the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred, measured in watts (W) = joules/second. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Average power"?

options.text: ½mv₁² - GMm/r₁ = ½mv₂² - GMm/r₂; used to find speed at any orbital distance without knowing forces
A system of two masses connected by a string over a pulley; a = (m₁ - m₂)g/(m₁ + m₂) for an ideal massless pulley
P_avg = W/Δt = ΔE/Δt; total work or energy transfer divided by elapsed time
When τ_net,ext = 0: I₁ω₁ = I₂ω₂; if I decreases, ω increases proportionally

quiz_questions.explanation: "Average power" is defined as: P_avg = W/Δt = ΔE/Δt; total work or energy transfer divided by elapsed time. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Instantaneous power"?

options.text: I = Σm_i r_i²; sum of each particle's mass times the square of its distance from the rotation axis
U = ½kx²; the energy stored in a spring displaced x from equilibrium
P = F·v = Fv cosθ; the power delivered at a specific instant when force F acts on an object moving at velocity v
τ = -mgd sinθ for a pendulum; for small θ, τ ≈ -mgdθ, giving angular SHM with ω = √(mgd/I)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Instantaneous power" is defined as: P = F·v = Fv cosθ; the power delivered at a specific instant when force F acts on an object moving at velocity v. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Efficiency"?

options.text: T² = (4π²/GM)r³; orbital period squared is proportional to orbital radius cubed
A·B = ABcosθ = AxBx + AyBy; a scalar measuring the projection of one vector onto another; used in calculating work
ω = ω₀ + αt, θ = θ₀ + ω₀t + ½αt², ω² = ω₀² + 2α(θ-θ₀); for constant α
η = P_out/P_in = W_useful/W_total; the fraction of input energy converted to useful work; always less than 1 due to losses

quiz_questions.explanation: "Efficiency" is defined as: η = P_out/P_in = W_useful/W_total; the fraction of input energy converted to useful work; always less than 1 due to losses. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Horsepower"?

options.text: ΣF = 0 and Στ = 0; conditions for an object to remain at rest with no linear or rotational acceleration
F = -kx; the restoring force of an ideal spring is proportional to displacement from equilibrium and directed opposite to it
1 hp = 746 W; a unit of power commonly used for engines and motors
F_net = ma or F_net = dp/dt; the net force equals mass times acceleration, or equivalently the rate of change of momentum

quiz_questions.explanation: "Horsepower" is defined as: 1 hp = 746 W; a unit of power commonly used for engines and motors. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "P = dW/dt = F·v; the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred, measured in watts (W) = joules/second"?

options.text: Total energy in orbit
Kepler's Third Law
Power
Potential energy function

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Power".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "P_avg = W/Δt = ΔE/Δt; total work or energy transfer divided by elapsed time"?

options.text: Parallel axis theorem
Free-body diagram
Average power
Vertical circle dynamics

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Average power".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "P = F·v = Fv cosθ; the power delivered at a specific instant when force F acts on an object moving at velocity v"?

options.text: Superposition
Net force
Linear momentum
Instantaneous power

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Instantaneous power".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "η = P_out/P_in = W_useful/W_total; the fraction of input energy converted to useful work; always less than 1 due to losses"?

options.text: Efficiency
Elastic PE
Impulse approximation
Dot product

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Efficiency".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "1 hp = 746 W; a unit of power commonly used for engines and motors"?

options.text: Tension
Horsepower
Center of mass reference frame
Static equilibrium

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Horsepower".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Power and Efficiency is accurate?

options.text: When non-conservative forces (friction, air resistance) are present, the generalized equation becomes KE₁ + PE₁ + W_nc = KE₂ + PE₂, where W_nc represents energy converted to or from thermal energy.
AP Physics C emphasizes setting up and evaluating these integrals.
Power and Efficiency describe the rate of energy transfer and the fraction of input energy converted to useful output.
A mass on a spring executing SHM with ω = √(k/m) demonstrates how periodic oscillations at a source generate traveling waves when coupled to a medium.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Power and Efficiency as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Power and Efficiency?

options.text: When non-conservative forces (friction, air resistance) are present, the generalized equation becomes KE₁ + PE₁ + W_nc = KE₂ + PE₂, where W_nc represents energy converted to or from thermal energy.
Power is defined as P = dW/dt = F·v (in watts), connecting force, velocity, and energy transfer rate.
Torque about a pivot determines whether an object rotates and in which direction.
The impulse-momentum theorem (J = ∫F dt = Δp) connects the time integral of force to the change in momentum, making it especially useful for analyzing collisions and impacts.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Power and Efficiency as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Power and Efficiency?

options.text: Efficiency η = useful energy output / total energy input ranges from 0 to 1 and is limited by friction, heat losses, and the laws of thermodynamics.
The simple pendulum (point mass on a massless string) has period T = 2π√(L/g) for small angles, independent of mass and amplitude.

Torque and Rotational Dynamics extend Newton's Second Law to rotation: τ_net = Iα, where torque τ = r × F (magnitude rF sinθ) is the rotational analog of force, and I (moment of inertia) is the rotational analog of mass.
A mass on a spring executing SHM with ω = √(k/m) demonstrates how periodic oscillations at a source generate traveling waves when coupled to a medium.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Power and Efficiency as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Power and Efficiency?

options.text: Power analysis is critical for engine design, electrical systems, and athletic performance.
For variable forces, the impulse is the area under the force-time graph.
The center of mass moves as if all the system's mass were concentrated there with F_ext = Ma_cm.
Work and Energy provide a scalar framework for analyzing motion without tracking forces and accelerations over time.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Power and Efficiency as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Power and Efficiency?

options.text: Power and Efficiency describe the rate of energy transfer and the fraction of input energy converted to useful output.
AP Physics C emphasizes calculus-based kinematics, using derivatives and integrals to describe motion with non-constant acceleration.
The gravitational field g = GM/r² at distance r from a mass M gives the local acceleration due to gravity, explaining why g varies with altitude and location on Earth.
Force-potential energy diagrams reveal equilibrium positions (where F = -dU/dx = 0) and their stability (slope determines restoring or repelling character).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Power and Efficiency as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Power and Efficiency?

options.text: Engineering spring-loaded mechanisms (car bumpers, mattresses) by calculating work stored and released using W = ½kx²
Orbital mechanics where gravitational force provides the centripetal force: mg = mv²/r leads to orbital velocity v = √(gr) for low orbits
Automobile engine specifications using power curves (horsepower vs. RPM) to match engine performance to vehicle weight and desired acceleration
Tidal force analysis showing how the Moon's gravity varies across Earth's diameter, creating two tidal bulges on opposite sides

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Power and Efficiency.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Power and Efficiency?

options.text: Calculating the energy required for rocket launches: the difference in gravitational PE between Earth's surface and the desired orbital altitude plus the kinetic energy needed
Calculating the power required for an elevator motor to lift a loaded car at constant speed: P = mgv
Door handle placement: handles are at the edge (maximum r) to maximize torque for a given applied force, making doors easy to open
Automotive suspension system analysis modeling shock absorbers as damped SHM to optimize ride comfort and handling characteristics

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Power and Efficiency.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Power and Efficiency applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Space elevator feasibility studies computing the potential energy change of lifting payload from Earth's surface to geosynchronous altitude versus traditional rocket propulsion
Analyzing inclined conveyor belt forces in mining operations to determine motor power needed to transport ore uphill
Calculating the energy required for rocket launches: the difference in gravitational PE between Earth's surface and the desired orbital altitude plus the kinetic energy needed
Athletic performance analysis measuring power output of cyclists and runners to optimize training and predict race performance

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Power and Efficiency.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Power and Efficiency?

options.text: Designing pulley systems (block and tackle) to provide mechanical advantage for lifting heavy loads in construction
Foucault pendulum demonstration proving Earth's rotation by observing the slow precession of a long pendulum's swing plane over hours
Automobile engine specifications using power curves (horsepower vs. RPM) to match engine performance to vehicle weight and desired acceleration
Figure skating spins: pulling arms in reduces I, increasing ω to conserve angular momentum (demonstrating I's physical significance)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Power and Efficiency.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Power and Efficiency in practice?

options.text: Vehicle accident reconstruction using partially inelastic collision models and coefficient of restitution to determine pre-collision speeds
Engineering centrifuge design calculating the g-forces (a_c = rω²) experienced by samples in medical and materials science applications
Orbital mechanics where gravitational force provides the centripetal force: mg = mv²/r leads to orbital velocity v = √(gr) for low orbits
Calculating the power required for an elevator motor to lift a loaded car at constant speed: P = mgv

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Power and Efficiency.
